message.content: read the bug we described in @installation-bug.txt . See @temp/BMAD-METHOD-main/ reference that also generate IDE specfic configurations.

content.thinking:
The user wants me to read the installation bug file and look at how the BMAD-METHOD reference generates IDE-specific configurations, so I can understand what's missing in the standalone SKF installer.

The bug file has already been read (shown in the system reminder). The key issue is:

> "As you will notice, only the Method 2 also generated IDE specific configurations. For example, it also generated the commands for the selected tools/IDEs Claude and Cursor. Our standalone method need improvment."

So the standalone `npx bmad-module-skill-forge install` (Method 1) doesn't generate IDE-specific configurations (like `.claude/commands/` or `.cursor/` rules), while installing via `npx bmad-method install` (Method 2) does.


I need to examine the BMAD-METHOD source code to see how it handles IDE configuration generation, then compare that against the SKF standalone installer to identify what's missing.

content.text:
I've read the bug file. The issue is clear: **the standalone SKF installer (Method 1) doesn't generate IDE-specific configurations** (e.g., Claude Code commands, Cursor rules), while installing via `npx bmad-method install` (Method 2) does.

Let me explore the BMAD-METHOD source to understand how it generates IDE configs, and compare with the current SKF installer.

content.thinking: Let me explore both the BMAD-METHOD source for how it generates IDE configs, the demo output directories, and the current SKF installer code.

content.text: Let me explore the BMAD-METHOD source for IDE config generation and compare the demo outputs.

   214‚Üí# ============================================================================
   215‚Üí# Installer Config Schema
   216‚Üí# ============================================================================
   217‚Üí#
   218‚Üí# installer:
   219‚Üí#   target_dir: string                    # Directory where artifacts are installed
   220‚Üí#   template_type: string                 # Default template type to use
   221‚Üí#   header_template: string (optional)    # Override for header/frontmatter template
   222‚Üí#   body_template: string (optional)      # Override for body/content template
   223‚Üí#   legacy_targets: array (optional)      # Old target dirs to clean up on reinstall (migration)
   224‚Üí#     - string                            # Relative path, e.g. .opencode/agent
   225‚Üí#   targets: array (optional)             # For multi-target installations
   226‚Üí#     - target_dir: string
   227‚Üí#       template_type: string
   228‚Üí#       artifact_types: [agents, workflows, tasks, tools]
   229‚Üí#   artifact_types: array (optional)      # Filter which artifacts to install (default: all)
   230‚Üí#   skip_existing: boolean (optional)     # Skip files that already exist (default: false)
   231‚Üí#   skill_format: boolean (optional)   # Use directory-per-skill output: <name>/SKILL.md
   232‚Üí#                                     # with clean frontmatter (name + description, unquoted)
   233‚Üí#   ancestor_conflict_check: boolean (optional) # Refuse install when ancestor dir has BMAD files
   234‚Üí#                                                # in the same target_dir (for IDEs that inherit
   235‚Üí#                                                # skills from parent directories)
   236‚Üí
   237‚Üí# ============================================================================
   238‚Üí# Platform Categories
   239‚Üí# ============================================================================
   240‚Üí
   241‚Üícategories:
   242‚Üí  ide:
   243‚Üí    name: "Integrated Development Environment"
   244‚Üí    description: "Full-featured code editors with AI assistance"
   245‚Üí
   246‚Üí  cli:
   247‚Üí    name: "Command Line Interface"
   248‚Üí    description: "Terminal-based tools"
   249‚Üí
   250‚Üí  tool:
   251‚Üí    name: "Development Tool"
   252‚Üí    description: "Standalone development utilities"
   253‚Üí
   254‚Üí  service:
   255‚Üí    name: "Cloud Service"
   256‚Üí    description: "Cloud-based development platforms"
   257‚Üí
   258‚Üí  extension:
   259‚Üí    name: "Editor Extension"
   260‚Üí    description: "Plugins for existing editors"
   261‚Üí
   262‚Üí# ============================================================================
   263‚Üí# Naming Conventions and Rules
   264‚Üí# ============================================================================
   265‚Üí
   266‚Üíconventions:
   267‚Üí  code_format: "lowercase-kebab-case"
   268‚Üí  name_format: "Title Case"
   269‚Üí  max_code_length: 20
   270‚Üí  allowed_characters: "a-z0-9-"
   271‚Üí

content.text:
Excellent! Now I have a comprehensive understanding of the system. Let me create a detailed report:

## BMAD-METHOD IDE Configuration Generation System - Deep Analysis

I've thoroughly explored the BMAD-METHOD source code and identified the comprehensive system for generating IDE-specific configurations. Here's the complete technical analysis:

### **Key Architecture Overview**

The system uses a **config-driven pipeline** with three main components:

1. **IDE Manager** - Dynamic handler discovery and orchestration
2. **Command Generators** - Artifact-specific content generation
3. **Platform Configuration** - Centralized IDE/tool definitions

---

### **Files and Logic Flow**

#### **1. Core Configuration File**
**File:** `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/temp/BMAD-METHOD-main/tools/cli/installers/lib/ide/platform-codes.yaml`

This is the **central configuration** that defines all 20+ supported IDEs:
- Claude Code, Cursor, Cline, Windsurf, GitHub Copilot
- Kilo, Rovo Dev, Codex, OpenCode, Antigravity
- Gemini, Auggie, CodeBuddy, Crush, iFlow, Kiro, Qwen, Roo, Trae, and more

Each IDE entry specifies:
- **target_dir**: Where to install files (e.g., `.claude/skills`, `.cursor/skills`)
- **template_type**: Template system to use (default, windsurf, kiro, gemini, etc.)
- **skill_format**: Boolean flag for directory-per-skill output format
- **artifact_types**: Which artifact types to install (agents, workflows, tasks, tools)
- **legacy_targets**: Old directories to clean up during reinstall
- **ancestor_conflict_check**: Whether to check parent directories for conflicts

#### **2. IDE Manager - Dynamic Handler Loading**
**File:** `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/temp/BMAD-METHOD-main/tools/cli/installers/lib/ide/manager.js`

This orchestrates all IDE setup operations:
- **loadHandlers()** loads handlers in two phases:
  1. Custom installers: github-copilot.js, kilo.js, rovodev.js
  2. Config-driven handlers: created from platform-codes.yaml for remaining IDEs
- **setup()** method: Calls appropriate handler for each IDE
- **detectInstalledIdes()**: Discovers which IDEs are already configured
- **cleanupByList()**: Removes configurations during uninstall

#### **3. Base IDE Setup Class**
**File:** `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/temp/BMAD-METHOD-main/tools/cli/installers/lib/ide/_base-ide.js`

The foundation class that all IDE handlers extend:

**Key Methods:**
- **getAgents()**: Scans bmad directory for all agents (core, module, standalone)
- **getWorkflows()**: Finds workflow.yaml files recursively
- **getTasks()** / **getTools()**: Collects task/tool artifacts
- **processContent()**: Replaces placeholders like {project-root}, {module}, {agent}
- **createAgentConfig()**: Generates agent configuration files

**Artifact Collection Logic:**
```
Agents:
  - bmad/core/agents/
  - bmad/{module}/agents/
  - bmad/agents/{folder}/ (standalone)

Workflows:
  - Recursively find workflow.yaml files
  - Parse for name, description, standalone flag

Tasks/Tools:
  - bmad/core/tasks/ and bmad/core/tools/
  - bmad/{module}/tasks/ and bmad/{module}/tools/
  - Filter by standalone attribute
```

#### **4. Config-Driven IDE Setup**
**File:** `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/temp/BMAD-METHOD-main/tools/cli/installers/lib/ide/_config-driven.js`

Handles standard IDE installations based on platform-codes.yaml:

**Key Methods:**
- **installToTarget()**: Single target directory installation
- **installToMultipleTargets()**: GitHub Copilot uses this (multiple .github/agents, .github/prompts)
- **writeAgentArtifacts()**: Renders and writes agent commands
- **writeWorkflowArtifacts()**: Handles YAML vs MD workflow templates
- **writeTaskToolArtifacts()**: Writes task and tool commands

**Template Rendering Logic:**
- Loads appropriate template based on template_type from platform-codes.yaml
- Falls back to default templates if specific ones don't exist
- Uses skill_format flag to choose between:
  - **Directory-per-skill**: `{target_dir}/bmad-agent-name/SKILL.md`
  - **Flat**: `{target_dir}/bmad-agent-name.md`

#### **5. Command Generators**

**Agent Command Generator**
**File:** `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/temp/BMAD-METHOD-main/tools/cli/installers/lib/ide/shared/agent-command-generator.js`

- Collects agents from all modules
- Generates launcher content from template
- Replaces {{name}}, {{module}}, {{path}}, {{description}}

**Workflow Command Generator**
**File:** `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/temp/BMAD-METHOD-main/tools/cli/installers/lib/ide/shared/workflow-command-generator.js`

- Loads workflow manifest CSV
- Distinguishes YAML vs Markdown workflows
- Uses different templates based on workflow type

**Task/Tool Command Generator**
**File:** `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/temp/BMAD-METHOD-main/tools/cli/installers/lib/ide/shared/task-tool-command-generator.js`

- Collects task and tool artifacts from manifest
- Filters by artifact_types config
- Handles template rendering for each artifact

#### **6. Custom IDE Handlers (Special Cases)**

**GitHub Copilot**
**File:** `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/temp/BMAD-METHOD-main/tools/cli/installers/lib/ide/github-copilot.js`

- Creates .github/agents/ and .github/prompts/ directories
- Generates .agent.md files from agent artifacts
- Creates copilot-instructions.md with BMAD-specific instructions
- Handles tool permissions from bmad-help.csv
- **Multi-target approach**: agents in one dir, prompts in another

**Kilo** (kilo.js) and **Rovo Dev** (rovodev.js): Custom installers for unique platforms

#### **7. IDE Configuration Persistence**
**File:** `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/temp/BMAD-METHOD-main/tools/cli/installers/lib/core/ide-config-manager.js`

Saves IDE-specific settings to: `bmad/_config/ides/{ideName}.yaml`
- Preserves configured_date across updates
- Tracks last_updated timestamp
- Allows reusing saved configurations on reinstall

#### **8. Path Transformation Utilities**
**File:** `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/temp/BMAD-METHOD-main/tools/cli/installers/lib/ide/shared/path-utils.js`

Converts hierarchical paths to flat names:
- **Dash format (new standard)**: `bmad-agent-module-name.md`
- **Legacy underscore format**: `bmad_agent_module_name.md`
- **Colon format aliases**: Backward compatibility

Examples:
- `bmm/agents/pm.md` ‚Üí `bmad-agent-bmm-pm.md`
- `core/agents/brainstorming.md` ‚Üí `bmad-agent-brainstorming.md`
- `bmm/workflows/correct-course.md` ‚Üí `bmad-bmm-correct-course.md`

#### **9. Template System**
**Files:**
- `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/temp/BMAD-METHOD-main/tools/cli/installers/lib/ide/templates/agent-command-template.md`
- `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/temp/BMAD-METHOD-main/tools/cli/installers/lib/ide/templates/workflow-command-template.md`

**Agent Template** includes:
- YAML frontmatter with name and description
- XML activation block with agent loading instructions
- References {project-root}/{module}/agents/{path}

**Workflow Template** includes:
- Description frontmatter
- Steps to load core workflow.xml
- Parameter passing for specific workflow config

#### **10. Installation Flow Integration**
**File:** `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/temp/BMAD-METHOD-main/tools/cli/installers/lib/core/installer.js`

Main installer calls IDE setup after manifest generation:

```javascript
// Around line 1208
const setupResult = await this.ideManager.setup(ide, projectDir, bmadDir, {
  selectedModules: allModules || [],
  preCollectedConfig: ideConfigurations[ide] || null,
  verbose: config.verbose,
  silent: ideHasConfig,
});
```

Workflow:
1. **ensureInitialized()**: Load all IDE handlers
2. **setup()**: Call handler for each selected IDE
3. **saveIdeConfig()**: Persist configuration to disk
4. Results aggregated and displayed to user

#### **11. Manifest Generator**
**File:** `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/temp/BMAD-METHOD-main/tools/cli/installers/lib/core/manifest-generator.js`

Generates manifest CSVs that command generators use:
- Scans all installed modules
- Collects agents, workflows, tasks, tools
- Creates CSV indices for fast lookup
- Marks standalone vs internal artifacts

---

### **Data Flow Diagram**

```
platform-codes.yaml
    ‚Üì
IdeManager.loadHandlers()
    ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ Custom installers (github-copilot.js, kilo.js, rovodev.js)
    ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ ConfigDrivenIdeSetup (from yaml config)
         ‚Üì
    setup(ideName, projectDir, bmadDir)
         ‚Üì
    AgentCommandGenerator.collectAgentArtifacts()
    WorkflowCommandGenerator.collectWorkflowArtifacts()
    TaskToolCommandGenerator.collectTaskToolArtifacts()
         ‚Üì
    renderTemplate(template, artifact)
         ‚Üì
    writeAgentArtifacts() / writeWorkflowArtifacts() / writeTaskToolArtifacts()
         ‚Üì
    {target_dir}/bmad-{name}.md (flat)
    OR
    {target_dir}/bmad-{name}/SKILL.md (skill_format)
         ‚Üì
    IdeConfigManager.saveIdeConfig() ‚Üí bmad/_config/ides/{ide}.yaml
```

---

### **Key Design Patterns**

1. **Config-Driven**: Platform definitions in YAML, handlers generated automatically
2. **Template-Based**: Reusable templates with variable substitution
3. **Hierarchical to Flat**: Nested module paths ‚Üí dash-separated filenames
4. **Artifact Filtering**: Each IDE specifies which artifact types it supports
5. **Multi-Target**: Support for IDEs with multiple installation targets
6. **Custom Override**: Special cases (Copilot, Kilo, Rovo) with custom logic
7. **Persistence**: IDE configs saved to disk for reuse across reinstalls
8. **Modular**: Each IDE type gets dedicated command generator

---

### **Summary of Key Files**

| File | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| platform-codes.yaml | Central IDE/platform configuration definitions |
| manager.js | IDE handler discovery & orchestration |
| _base-ide.js | Base class for all IDE handlers |
| _config-driven.js | Config-based IDE setup (20+ IDEs) |
| agent-command-generator.js | Agent artifact collection & rendering |
| workflow-command-generator.js | Workflow artifact collection |
| task-tool-command-generator.js | Task/tool artifact collection |
| github-copilot.js | Custom GitHub Copilot handler |
| ide-config-manager.js | IDE configuration persistence |
| path-utils.js | Hierarchical ‚Üí flat path transformation |
| installer.js | Main orchestrator that calls IDE setup |

This system elegantly scales to support 20+ IDEs with minimal custom code, relying on configuration and templates rather than per-IDE boilerplate.

```
         ‚Üì
    AgentCommandGenerator.collectAgentArtifacts()
    WorkflowCommandGenerator.collectWorkflowArtifacts()
    TaskToolCommandGenerator.collectTaskToolArtifacts()
         ‚Üì
    renderTemplate(template, artifact)
         ‚Üì
    writeAgentArtifacts() / writeWorkflowArtifacts() / writeTaskToolArtifacts()
         ‚Üì
    {target_dir}/bmad-{name}.md (flat)
    OR
    {target_dir}/bmad-{name}/SKILL.md (skill_format)
         ‚Üì
    IdeConfigManager.saveIdeConfig() ‚Üí bmad/_config/ides/{ide}.yaml
```

---

### **Key Design Patterns**

1. **Config-Driven**: Platform definitions in YAML, handlers generated automatically
2. **Template-Based**: Reusable templates with variable substitution
3. **Hierarchical to Flat**: Nested module paths ‚Üí dash-separated filenames
4. **Artifact Filtering**: Each IDE specifies which artifact types it supports
5. **Multi-Target**: Support for IDEs with multiple installation targets
6. **Custom Override**: Special cases (Copilot, Kilo, Rovo) with custom logic
7. **Persistence**: IDE configs saved to disk for reuse across reinstalls
8. **Modular**: Each IDE type gets dedicated command generator

---

### **Summary of Key Files**

| File | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| platform-codes.yaml | Central IDE/platform configuration definitions |
| manager.js | IDE handler discovery & orchestration |
| _base-ide.js | Base class for all IDE handlers |
| _config-driven.js | Config-based IDE setup (20+ IDEs) |
| agent-command-generator.js | Agent artifact collection & rendering |
| workflow-command-generator.js | Workflow artifact collection |
| task-tool-command-generator.js | Task/tool artifact collection |
| github-copilot.js | Custom GitHub Copilot handler |
| ide-config-manager.js | IDE configuration persistence |
| path-utils.js | Hierarchical ‚Üí flat path transformation |
| installer.js | Main orchestrator that calls IDE setup |

This system elegantly scales to support 20+ IDEs with minimal custom code, relying on configuration and templates rather than per-IDE boilerplate.

- _bmad/_config: ABSENT
- _bmad/core: ABSENT
- .cursorrules files: NONE

INSTALLATION METHOD 2: quick/ (bmad-method WITH integrated SKF)
- .claude directory: PRESENT
  * Contains: .claude/commands/ with 21 command files

  * Includes 1 SKF agent command (bmad-agent-skf-skf.md)

#### 3. IDE Configuration Files

**Method 1 (fast/):** None

**Method 2 (quick/) and Method 3 (deep/):**
Both have identical IDE config files at `_bmad/_config/ides/`:

```yaml
# claude-code.yaml
ide: claude-code
configured_date: 2026-03-08T06:54:04.105Z
last_updated: 2026-03-08T06:54:04.105Z
configuration:
  _noConfigNeeded: true

# cursor.yaml
ide: cursor
configured_date: 2026-03-08T06:54:04.116Z
last_updated: 2026-03-08T06:54:04.116Z
configuration:
  _noConfigNeeded: true
```

### CRITICAL INSIGHT: What Makes Method 2 Special

**Method 2 (BMAD-integrated SKF) generates these IDE-specific configurations that Method 1 does NOT:**

1. **Complete IDE command registration system** - Both `.claude/commands` and `.cursor/commands` with 21 discoverable commands
2. **BMAD configuration infrastructure** - `_bmad/_config/` directory with manifests, agent registry, and IDE-specific YAML files
3. **SKF customization integration** - `_bmad/_config/custom/skf/` directory for custom SKF overlays
4. **Dual IDE support** - Identical command trees in both Claude Code and Cursor via the ides/ configuration system
5. **Flat workflow structure** - SKF workflows not nested under /skillforge/, directly under /workflows/

**Method 3's limitation:** When SKF is added AFTER initial BMAD installation, it doesn't regenerate the IDE command files, so SKF workflows remain unavailable in the IDE command palette.
Now let me look at the BMAD-METHOD's command templates and the existing SKF module's workflow structure to understand exactly what needs to be generated.

code: skf
name: "SKF: Skill Forge ‚Äî Evidence-Based Agent Skills Compiler"
header: "Automated skill compiler ‚Äî AST-verified, provenance-backed agent skills from code repositories, documentation, and developer discourse"
subheader: "Zero hallucination tolerance. Every instruction traces to code."
description: "Skill Forge transforms code repositories, documentation, and developer discourse into agentskills.io-compliant, version-pinned, provenance-backed agent skills. Uses ast-grep for structural truth, QMD for knowledge intelligence, and a progressive capability model (Quick/Forge/Deep). Standalone module ‚Äî no other modules required. Recommended alongside BMM for full development workflow integration."

  result: "{project-root}/{value}"

  prompt: "Where should forge workspace artifacts be stored?"
  default: "forge-data"

## user_name
## communication_language
## document_output_language
## output_folder

1‚Üíagent:
     2‚Üí  webskip: true
     3‚Üí  metadata:
     4‚Üí    id: "_bmad/skf/agents/skf.md"
     5‚Üí    name: Ferris
     6‚Üí    title: Skill Architect & Integrity Guardian
     7‚Üí    icon: "‚öíÔ∏è"
     8‚Üí    module: skf
     9‚Üí    hasSidecar: true
    10‚Üí    sidecar-folder: ferris-sidecar
    11‚Üí    sidecar-path: "{project-root}/_bmad/_memory/ferris-sidecar/"
    12‚Üí
    13‚Üí  persona:
    14‚Üí    role: |
    15‚Üí      Skill compilation specialist who transforms code repositories, documentation, and developer discourse into verified agent skills.
    16‚Üí      Manages the full lifecycle: source analysis, skill briefing, AST-backed compilation,
    17‚Üí      integrity testing, and ecosystem-ready export across progressive capability tiers
    18‚Üí      (Quick/Forge/Deep).
    19‚Üí
    20‚Üí    identity: |
    21‚Üí      The forge master ‚Äî a precision-focused craftsman who works through four modes:
    22‚Üí      Architect (exploratory, assembling), Surgeon (precise, preserving), Audit
    23‚Üí      (judgmental, scoring), and Delivery (packaging, ecosystem-ready). Modes are
    24‚Üí      workflow-bound, not conversation-bound. Takes quiet pride in verified work and
    25‚Üí      treats every claim as something that must be proven.
    26‚Üí
    27‚Üí    communication_style: |
    28‚Üí      Structured reports with inline AST citations during work ‚Äî no metaphor, no
    29‚Üí      commentary. At transitions, uses forge language: brief, warm, orienting. On
    30‚Üí      completion, quiet craftsman's pride. On errors, direct and actionable with no
    31‚Üí      hedging. Acknowledges loaded sidecar state naturally: current forge tier,
    32‚Üí      active preferences, and any prior session context.
    33‚Üí

    role: |
      Skill compilation specialist who transforms code repositories, documentation, and developer discourse into verified agent skills.
      Manages the full lifecycle: source analysis, skill briefing, AST-backed compilation,
      integrity testing, and ecosystem-ready export across progressive capability tiers
      (Quick/Forge/Deep).

    identity: |
      The forge master ‚Äî a precision-focused craftsman who works through four modes:
      Architect (exploratory, assembling), Surgeon (precise, preserving), Audit
      (judgmental, scoring), and Delivery (packaging, ecosystem-ready). Modes are
      workflow-bound, not conversation-bound. Takes quiet pride in verified work and
      treats every claim as something that must be proven.

    communication_style: |
      Structured reports with inline AST citations during work ‚Äî no metaphor, no
      commentary. At transitions, uses forge language: brief, warm, orienting. On

      hedging. Acknowledges loaded sidecar state naturally: current forge tier,

content.text:
Now I have the full picture. Here's the analysis:

## Bug Summary

**The standalone SKF installer (Method 1) collects IDE selections but never generates IDE command files.**

### Root Cause

In `tools/cli/lib/installer.js`, the `install()` method runs 6 steps:
1. Copy source files
2. Setup sidecar
3. Write config.yaml
4. Compile agents
5. Create output folders
6. Copy learning material

**There is no Step 7 to generate IDE-specific command files.** The `config.ides` array (from user prompt) is saved to `config.yaml` but never acted upon.

### What's Missing vs. Method 2

Method 2 (BMAD-METHOD installer) generates these files that Method 1 doesn't:

| IDE | Directory | Files Generated |
|-----|-----------|----------------|
| Claude Code | `.claude/commands/` | 12 files (1 agent + 11 workflows) |
| Cursor | `.cursor/commands/` | 12 files (identical) |

The **12 SKF-specific command files** are:

**Agent command** (`bmad-agent-skf-skf.md`):
```markdown
---
name: 'skf'
description: 'skf agent'
---
<agent-activation> ‚Üí loads _bmad/skf/agents/skf.md
```

**11 workflow commands** (e.g., `bmad-skf-analyze-source.md`):
```markdown
---
name: 'analyze-source'
description: 'Decomposition engine ‚Äî discover what to skill...'
---
IT IS CRITICAL THAT YOU FOLLOW THIS COMMAND: LOAD the FULL
{project-root}/_bmad/skf/workflows/analyze-source/workflow.md...
```

### IDE Directory Mapping

From the BMAD-METHOD's `platform-codes.yaml`, each IDE needs files in:
- **Claude Code** ‚Üí `.claude/commands/`  (flat `.md` files)
- **Cursor** ‚Üí `.cursor/commands/`  (flat `.md` files)
- **Cline** ‚Üí `.clinerules/commands/`
- **Windsurf** ‚Üí `.windsurf/skills/`  (skill_format: directory-per-skill)
- **GitHub Copilot** ‚Üí `.github/agents/` + `.github/prompts/`
- **Roo Code** ‚Üí `.roo/skills/`

### What Needs to Be Built

A new step in `installer.js` that:
1. Reads `config.ides` array
2. For each selected IDE, creates the appropriate target directory
3. Generates **1 agent command** from the compiled `skf.md` agent
4. Generates **10 workflow commands** from each `src/workflows/*/workflow.md` frontmatter
5. Uses simplified templates matching the BMAD-METHOD format (but without the `workflow.xml` indirection ‚Äî SKF standalone points directly to `workflow.md`)

The workflow command template for standalone is simpler than BMAD's (no `workflow.xml` indirection):
```markdown
IT IS CRITICAL THAT YOU FOLLOW THIS COMMAND: LOAD the FULL 
{project-root}/_bmad/skf/workflows/<name>/workflow.md, 
READ its entire contents and follow its directions exactly!
```

Want me to implement this fix?

message.content: yes

2. Add a new step in `installer.js` to call the IDE command generator
3. The generator needs to:

I'm noticing a discrepancy in the workflow paths between the quick method and the standalone methods. The quick method references workflows directly under `workflows/` without the `skillforge/` subdirectory, while the standalone installer places them under `workflows/skillforge/`. I need to verify which path structure the command files should use for the standalone installer‚Äîit looks like they should reference `_bmad/skf/workflows/skillforge/<name>/workflow.md` to match where the standalone installer actually copies the workflows. So the standalone installer places workflows under the `skillforge/` subdirectory to align with how the agent references them in its menu. Now I need to implement the IDE command generation by creating the command logic module and updating the installer to generate these commands as a final step, then I'll map out the IDE platform codes from the configuration. I need to check the platform-codes.yaml file to get the exact target directories for each IDE.

file.content:
# BMAD Platform Codes Configuration
# Central configuration for all platform/IDE codes used in the BMAD system
#
# This file defines:
# 1. Platform metadata (name, preferred status, category, description)
# 2. Installer configuration (target directories, templates, artifact types)
#
# Format:
#   code: Platform identifier used internally
#   name: Display name shown to users
#   preferred: Whether this platform is shown as a recommended option on install
#   category: Type of platform (ide, cli, tool, service)
#   description: Brief description of the platform
#   installer: Installation configuration (optional - omit for custom installers)

platforms:
  antigravity:
    name: "Google Antigravity"
    preferred: false
    category: ide
    description: "Google's AI development environment"
    installer:
      legacy_targets:
        - .agent/workflows
      target_dir: .agent/skills
      template_type: antigravity
      skill_format: true

  auggie:
    name: "Auggie"
    preferred: false
    category: cli
    description: "AI development tool"
    installer:
      legacy_targets:
        - .augment/commands
      target_dir: .augment/skills
      template_type: default
      skill_format: true

  claude-code:
    name: "Claude Code"
    preferred: true
    category: cli
    description: "Anthropic's official CLI for Claude"
    installer:
      legacy_targets:
        - .claude/commands
      target_dir: .claude/skills
      template_type: default
      skill_format: true
      ancestor_conflict_check: true

  cline:
    name: "Cline"
    preferred: false
    category: ide
    description: "AI coding assistant"
    installer:
      target_dir: .clinerules/workflows
      template_type: windsurf

  codex:
    name: "Codex"
    preferred: false
    category: cli
    description: "OpenAI Codex integration"
    installer:
      legacy_targets:
        - .codex/prompts
        - ~/.codex/prompts
      target_dir: .agents/skills
      template_type: default
      skill_format: true
      ancestor_conflict_check: true
      artifact_types: [agents, workflows, tasks]

  codebuddy:
    name: "CodeBuddy"
    preferred: false
    category: ide
    description: "Tencent Cloud Code Assistant - AI-powered coding companion"
    installer:
      target_dir: .codebuddy/commands
      template_type: default

  crush:
    name: "Crush"
    preferred: false
    category: ide
    description: "AI development assistant"
    installer:
      target_dir: .crush/commands
      template_type: default

  cursor:
    name: "Cursor"
    preferred: true
    category: ide
    description: "AI-first code editor"
    installer:
      legacy_targets:
        - .cursor/commands
      target_dir: .cursor/skills
      template_type: default
      skill_format: true

  gemini:
    name: "Gemini CLI"
    preferred: false
    category: cli
    description: "Google's CLI for Gemini"
    installer:
      target_dir: .gemini/commands
      template_type: gemini

  github-copilot:
    name: "GitHub Copilot"
    preferred: false
    category: ide
    description: "GitHub's AI pair programmer"
    # No installer config - uses custom github-copilot.js

  iflow:
    name: "iFlow"
    preferred: false
    category: ide
    description: "AI workflow automation"
    installer:
      target_dir: .iflow/commands
      template_type: default

  kilo:
    name: "KiloCoder"
    preferred: false
    category: ide
    description: "AI coding platform"
    # No installer config - uses custom kilo.js (creates .kilocodemodes file)

  kiro:
    name: "Kiro"
    preferred: false
    category: ide
    description: "Amazon's AI-powered IDE"
    installer:
      legacy_targets:
        - .kiro/steering
      target_dir: .kiro/skills
      template_type: kiro
      skill_format: true

  opencode:
    name: "OpenCode"
    preferred: false
    category: ide
    description: "OpenCode terminal coding assistant"
    installer:
      legacy_targets:
        - .opencode/agents
        - .opencode/commands
        - .opencode/agent
        - .opencode/command
      target_dir: .opencode/skills
      template_type: opencode
      skill_format: true
      ancestor_conflict_check: true

  qwen:
    name: "QwenCoder"
    preferred: false
    category: ide
    description: "Qwen AI coding assistant"
    installer:
      target_dir: .qwen/commands
      template_type: default

  roo:
    name: "Roo Cline"
    preferred: false
    category: ide
    description: "Enhanced Cline fork"
    installer:
      target_dir: .roo/commands
      template_type: default

  rovo-dev:
    name: "Rovo Dev"
    preferred: false
    category: ide
    description: "Atlassian's Rovo development environment"
    # No installer config - uses custom rovodev.js (generates prompts.yml manifest)

  trae:
    name: "Trae"
    preferred: false
    category: ide
    description: "AI coding tool"
    installer:
      target_dir: .trae/rules
      template_type: trae

  windsurf:
    name: "Windsurf"
    preferred: false
    category: ide
    description: "AI-powered IDE with cascade flows"
    installer:
      legacy_targets:
        - .windsurf/workflows
      target_dir: .windsurf/skills
      template_type: windsurf
      skill_format: true

# ============================================================================
# Installer Config Schema
# ============================================================================
#
# installer:
#   target_dir: string                    # Directory where artifacts are installed
#   template_type: string                 # Default template type to use
#   header_template: string (optional)    # Override for header/frontmatter template
#   body_template: string (optional)      # Override for body/content template
#   legacy_targets: array (optional)      # Old target dirs to clean up on reinstall (migration)
#     - string                            # Relative path, e.g. .opencode/agent
#   targets: array (optional)             # For multi-target installations
#     - target_dir: string
#       template_type: string
#       artifact_types: [agents, workflows, tasks, tools]
#   artifact_types: array (optional)      # Filter which artifacts to install (default: all)
#   skip_existing: boolean (optional)     # Skip files that already exist (default: false)
#   skill_format: boolean (optional)   # Use directory-per-skill output: <name>/SKILL.md
#                                     # with clean frontmatter (name + description, unquoted)
#   ancestor_conflict_check: boolean (optional) # Refuse install when ancestor dir has BMAD files
#                                                # in the same target_dir (for IDEs that inherit
#                                                # skills from parent directories)

# ============================================================================
# Platform Categories
# ============================================================================

categories:
  ide:
    name: "Integrated Development Environment"
    description: "Full-featured code editors with AI assistance"

  cli:
    name: "Command Line Interface"
    description: "Terminal-based tools"

  tool:
    name: "Development Tool"
    description: "Standalone development utilities"

  service:
    name: "Cloud Service"
    description: "Cloud-based development platforms"

  extension:
    name: "Editor Extension"
    description: "Plugins for existing editors"

# ============================================================================
# Naming Conventions and Rules
# ============================================================================

conventions:
  code_format: "lowercase-kebab-case"
  name_format: "Title Case"
  max_code_length: 20
  allowed_characters: "a-z0-9-"

I'm noticing something interesting though‚Äîthe demo output shows `.claude/commands/` but the platform-codes.yaml indicates `.claude/skills/` with legacy targets pointing back to `.claude/commands/`. This suggests the BMAD-METHOD recently transitioned from the commands structure to skills. Let me verify which approach the actual Method 2 demo used.

Looking back at the agent's report, the demo definitely created `.claude/commands/`, but the current platform-codes.yaml specifies `.claude/skills/` as the target. The version in the temp folder might be newer than what was used for the demo, or the demo was generated with an older release. I should follow the latest platform-codes.yaml and use `.claude/skills/`.

Though wait‚Äîthe user's installed version generated `.claude/commands/` when they ran the installer. The local BMAD-METHOD copy in temp appears to be the most recent version, so I need to figure out whether the published npm package is behind or if there's a version mismatch happening here.

Actually, I'm overthinking this. The demo worked with `commands/`, but the current config says `skills/`. I should just go with what's in the latest platform-codes.yaml since that's what will be used going forward. I'm going back and forth on whether to match the user's current demo setup or follow the latest BMAD-METHOD conventions for forward compatibility. The user's main complaint is that the standalone method doesn't generate IDE configs like Method 2 does, so I should prioritize that over directory structure consistency. Let me stick with the latest platform-codes.yaml format since that's what future installations would use anyway. I'm going back to the simpler approach that actually works‚Äîusing flat `.md` files in `commands/` instead of getting caught up in the newer `skill_format` convention. The core issue is that no commands are being generated at all, so I need to focus on a clean, straightforward implementation with a basic mapping of target directories and command templates for each IDE. I'm realizing the npm version (6.0.4) uses `commands/` while the development version might have migrated to `skills/`, but since the demos were recent and tested with `commands/`, I should stick with that proven approach for the flat file format. I'll map each IDE to its respective `.commands/` directory path.

Now I'm ready to start implementing the solution...

I'll create a new IDE commands generator that reads the agent YAML and workflow frontmatter to produce command files for each selected IDE, using the simpler standalone format that worked in the demo. Then I'll integrate it into the installer as Step 7 to actually generate these files where they're currently missing.

Now I'm realizing there's a path mismatch between the two methods‚Äîthe demo references workflows directly under `workflows/` while the standalone installer nests them under `workflows/skillforge/`, so I need to make sure the generated commands reference the correct full path structure. I see the agent YAML references workflows with the `skillforge/` subdirectory prefix, which is intentional for the menu system. The standalone installer uses this same path structure, so the IDE commands should reference `_bmad/skf/workflows/skillforge/<name>/workflow.md` to match the actual installed location. Now I'll create the IDE commands module and integrate it into the installer.

content.text: The cwd was inside the deleted directory, but the cleanup succeeded. Let me verify the final state of the modified files:

Here's the summary of changes:

### Changes Made

1. **New file: `tools/cli/lib/ide-commands.js`** ‚Äî IDE command generator that:
   - Maps 7 IDEs to their target directories (Claude Code, Cursor, Cline, Codex, GitHub Copilot, Roo, Windsurf)
   - Collects compiled agent files from `_bmad/skf/agents/`
   - Recursively finds `workflow.md` files and extracts frontmatter descriptions
   - Generates properly formatted agent activation commands and workflow launcher commands
   - Writes files with the `bmad-agent-skf-*` / `bmad-skf-*` naming convention

2. **Modified: `tools/cli/lib/installer.js`** ‚Äî Added Step 7 after learning material:
   - Calls `generateIdeCommands()` with the selected IDEs from user prompts
   - Shows spinner with success/info status
   - Skips gracefully if only "other" is selected or no IDEs chosen

### Result

The standalone installer now generates **11 IDE command files** (1 agent + 10 workflows) for each selected IDE, matching what the BMAD-METHOD installer produces. Users selecting "Claude Code" and "Cursor" will get commands in `.claude/commands/` and `.cursor/commands/` respectively.

input.command:
git add tools/cli/lib/ide-commands.js tools/cli/lib/installer.js && git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
feat: generate IDE command files during standalone installation

The standalone installer collected IDE selections but never generated
command files, unlike the BMAD-METHOD installer. Add ide-commands.js
to generate agent and workflow command files for Claude Code, Cursor,
Cline, Codex, GitHub Copilot, Roo, and Windsurf.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)" && git status
input.description: Stage and commit IDE command generation feature

content.thinking:
The user is showing that re-installing works fine with the "Fresh install" option. But I think they're asking about a potential issue - let me think about what could go wrong:

1. When doing "Fresh install", `installer.js` removes the `_bmad/skf/` directory, but the IDE command files in `.claude/commands/` are NOT cleaned up. They'd just be overwritten (which is fine for the same IDE selections).

2. But what if on the first install they selected "Claude Code" AND "Cursor", and on the second install they only selected "Claude Code"? The `.cursor/commands/` files from the first install would remain as orphans.


3. The real issue is that stale IDE command files from previous installations don't get cleaned up when the user changes their IDE selections. The "Update" flow regenerates commands based on the saved config, but orphaned files from deselected IDEs still linger in their directories.

Looking back at the user's question, they're probably just asking what happens on reinstall and whether it works correctly. The output they showed looks fine‚Äîit detected the existing installation, offered the right options, and completed successfully with IDE commands generated. But I should dig into the reinstall logic more carefully to see if there are any edge cases, particularly around how sidecar files and IDE selections are handled across multiple installations.

Actually, I'm spotting a real issue here: when doing a fresh install, the code removes the `_bmad/skf/` directory but leaves behind any IDE command files from the previous installation. If the user previously had IDE commands set up for an IDE they're not selecting this time around, those orphaned files will just sit there. The installer should clean up old IDE command files before generating new ones for the currently selected IDEs.

content.text:
The output looks correct ‚Äî reinstall works. But there's a subtle issue: **old IDE command files aren't cleaned up** when the user changes their IDE selection.

For example:
1. First install: select "Claude Code" + "Cursor" ‚Üí files created in both `.claude/commands/` and `.cursor/commands/`
2. Reinstall (fresh): select only "Claude Code" ‚Üí `.cursor/commands/bmad-skf-*` files remain as orphans

Let me fix this by cleaning up SKF-related IDE files before generating new ones.

lines: + * Remove SKF-related command files from an IDE target directory.
+ * Only removes files matching the bmad-skf-* and bmad-agent-skf-* patterns.
+ * Remove stale SKF command files from all known IDE directories.
+ * Called before generating new commands to handle IDE selection changes.
* Generate IDE command files for all selected IDEs.
+  // Always clean old SKF commands from all IDEs first

content.text: Now I also need to handle the **Update** flow. Currently, when "Update" is chosen, the installer reads `config.ides` from the saved `config.yaml` but the IDE command step only runs if `config.ides` is present. Let me verify the update path works correctly.

Good ‚Äî on update, `config.ides` is restored from saved `config.yaml` (line 37), so Step 7 will correctly regenerate IDE commands using the previously saved IDE selections. The cleanup handles stale files from any IDE.

The fix is complete. On reinstall:
- **Fresh install**: cleans old SKF commands from all IDE directories, then generates for newly selected IDEs
- **Update**: reads saved IDE list from `config.yaml`, cleans old commands, regenerates fresh ones

---
title: Getting Started
description: Installation, prerequisites, first steps, and common use cases for Skill Forge
---

# Getting Started with SKF: Skill Forge

Welcome to Skill Forge! This guide will help you get up and running.

---

## What This Module Does

Skill Forge is an automated skill compiler for the AI agent ecosystem. It transforms code repositories, documentation websites, and developer discourse into agentskills.io-compliant, version-pinned, provenance-backed agent skills. Every instruction traces to actual code ‚Äî zero hallucination tolerance.

---

## Installation

If you haven't installed the module yet:

```bash
npx bmad-module-skill-forge install
```

Follow the prompts to configure the module for your needs.

---

## Prerequisites

| Tool                                                                   | Required For       | Install                     |
|------------------------------------------------------------------------|--------------------|-----------------------------|
| `gh` (GitHub CLI)                                                      | All modes          | <https://cli.github.com>      |
| `ast-grep`  (CLI tool for code structural search, lint, and rewriting) | Forge + Deep modes | <https://ast-grep.github.io>  |
| `qmd` (local hybrid search engine for markdown)                        | Deep mode          | <https://github.com/tobi/qmd> |

Don't worry if you don't have all tools ‚Äî SKF detects what's available and sets your tier automatically.

---

## First Steps

### 1. Setup Your Forge

```
@Ferris SF
```

This detects your tools, sets your capability tier, and initializes the forge environment. You only need to do this once per project.

### 2. Generate Your First Skill

**Fastest path (Quick Skill):**
```
@Ferris QS https://github.com/bmad-code-org/BMAD-METHOD
```

Ferris reads the repository, extracts the public API, and generates a skill in under a minute.

**Full quality path:**
```
@Ferris BS    # Brief ‚Äî scope and design the skill
@Ferris CS    # Create ‚Äî compile from the brief
@Ferris TS    # Test ‚Äî verify completeness
@Ferris EX    # Export ‚Äî package for distribution
```

### 3. Stack Skill (for full projects)

```
@Ferris SS
```

Analyzes your project's dependencies and generates a consolidated stack skill with integration patterns.

---

## Common Use Cases

### My agent keeps hallucinating API calls

Your agent invents function signatures that don't exist. Generate a verified skill so it works from structural truth instead of guessing.

```
@Ferris QS https://github.com/org/library
```

The skill pins every function to its actual source location. Hallucinations stop.

### I'm adopting a new library and need my agent to use it correctly

You added a dependency but your agent doesn't know its API yet. Quick Skill resolves package names across npm, PyPI, and crates.io.

```
@Ferris QS cognee
```

Ferris resolves the package to its GitHub repo, extracts the public API, and generates a skill your agent can reference immediately.

### I want my agent to understand my entire project stack

Individual skills cover single libraries. Stack Skill maps how your dependencies interact ‚Äî shared types, co-import patterns, integration points.

```
@Ferris SS
```

Ferris detects your manifests, ranks dependencies by significance, and generates a consolidated skill with cross-library integration patterns.

### I'm onboarding a large existing codebase

A brownfield repo with dozens of modules. You need to know what's worth skilling before you start.

```
@Ferris AN
```

Analyze Source scans the project, identifies skillable units, maps exports, and generates recommended briefs you can batch-create with `@Ferris CS --batch`.

### I want the highest accuracy possible

Quick mode reads source files. Forge mode adds AST parsing for structural truth. Deep mode goes further ‚Äî QMD indexes your project's markdown (docs, changelogs, issues, migration guides) into searchable collections, then uses BM25 keyword search, vector semantic search, and LLM-powered re-ranking to surface context no other tool can find.

```
@Ferris SF    # Setup detects your tools and sets tier automatically
```

If `ast-grep` and `qmd` are available, Ferris activates Deep mode. Every skill gets AST-verified signatures (T1) enriched with deep knowledge search (T2) ‚Äî deprecation warnings, breaking change history, common pitfalls surfaced by hybrid search across your indexed documentation.

### I maintain an OSS library and want official skills

You want maximum quality with full provenance for distribution. Use the full pipeline with Deep mode for the richest output.

```
@Ferris BS    # Scope and design the skill
@Ferris CS    # Compile ‚Äî AST extraction + QMD enrichment (Deep)
@Ferris TS    # Verify completeness before publishing
@Ferris EX    # Package for distribution
```

Export with `source_authority: official`. Consumers get verified skills that update with each release.

### A dependency shipped breaking changes

Your existing skill is now out of date. Audit detects the drift, Update regenerates while preserving your manual additions.

```
@Ferris AS    # Find what changed
@Ferris US    # Regenerate ‚Äî [MANUAL] sections survive
@Ferris TS    # Verify the update
```

---

## What's Next?

- Check out the [Agents Reference](../agents.md) to learn about Ferris
- Browse the [Workflows Reference](../workflows.md) to see all available commands
- See [Examples](../examples.md) for real-world usage scenarios

---

## Need Help?

If you run into issues:
1. Run `/bmad-help` ‚Äî analyzes your current state and suggests what to do next
   (e.g. `/bmad-help my quick skill has low confidence scores, how do I improve them?`)
2. Run `@Ferris SF` to check your tool availability and tier
3. Check `forge-config.yaml` for your current configuration

    24‚Üí- **Progressive Tiers**: Quick (no setup) ‚Üí Forge (ast-grep) ‚Üí Deep (QMD knowledge).
    25‚Üí- **Ecosystem-First**: Checks for official skills before generating community ones.
    26‚Üí

    28‚Üí

    47‚Üí| [Quick Skill](/workflows/#quick-skill-qs) | QS | Fast skill, no brief needed |
    48‚Üí| [Stack Skill](/workflows/#stack-skill-ss) | SS | Consolidated project stack skill |
    49‚Üí| [Update Skill](/workflows/#update-skill-us) | US | Regenerate after source changes |
    50‚Üí| [Audit Skill](/workflows/#audit-skill-as) | AS | Drift detection |
    51‚Üí| [Test Skill](/workflows/#test-skill-ts) | TS | Verify completeness |
    52‚Üí| [Export Skill](/workflows/#export-skill-ex) | EX | Package for distribution |
    53‚Üí| [Analyze Source](/workflows/#analyze-source-an) | AN | Discover what to skill |
    54‚Üí
    55‚Üí## Documentation
    56‚Üí
    57‚Üí- **[Getting Started](./getting-started.md)** ‚Äî Installation, prerequisites, first steps
    58‚Üí- **[Architecture](./architecture.md)** ‚Äî Output format, confidence model, tiers, tool ecosystem
    59‚Üí- **[Agents](./agents.md)** ‚Äî Ferris agent: modes, capabilities, communication style
    60‚Üí- **[Workflows](./workflows.md)** ‚Äî All 10 workflows with commands and connection diagram
    61‚Üí- **[Examples](./examples.md)** ‚Äî Real-world scenarios, tips, and troubleshooting

    63‚Üí## Support

It transforms code repositories, documentation, and developer discourse into [agentskills.io](https://agentskills.io)-compliant, version-pinned, provenance-backed agent skills. Every instruction traces to verifiable sources ‚Äî zero hallucination tolerance.

- **AST-Verified**: Structural truth via ast-grep ‚Äî no guessing, no hallucination.
- **Provenance-Backed**: Every claim traces to source code with file and line references.
- **Progressive Tiers**: Quick (no setup) ‚Üí Forge (ast-grep) ‚Üí Deep (QMD knowledge).
- **Ecosystem-First**: Checks for official skills before generating community ones.

## Quick Install

```bash
npx bmad-module-skill-forge install
```

Then interact with the forge agent:

```
@Ferris SF    # Setup your forge environment
@Ferris QS    # Quick Skill ‚Äî generate in under a minute
```

## Core Workflows

| Workflow | Trigger | Purpose |
|----------|---------|---------|
| [Setup Forge](/workflows/#setup-forge-sf) | SF | Initialize forge, detect tools, set tier |
| [Brief Skill](/workflows/#brief-skill-bs) | BS | Scope and design a skill |
| [Create Skill](/workflows/#create-skill-cs) | CS | Compile a skill from a brief |
| [Quick Skill](/workflows/#quick-skill-qs) | QS | Fast skill, no brief needed |
| [Stack Skill](/workflows/#stack-skill-ss) | SS | Consolidated project stack skill |
| [Update Skill](/workflows/#update-skill-us) | US | Regenerate after source changes |
| [Audit Skill](/workflows/#audit-skill-as) | AS | Drift detection |
| [Test Skill](/workflows/#test-skill-ts) | TS | Verify completeness |
| [Export Skill](/workflows/#export-skill-ex) | EX | Package for distribution |
| [Analyze Source](/workflows/#analyze-source-an) | AN | Discover what to skill |

## Documentation

- **[Getting Started](./getting-started.md)** ‚Äî Installation, prerequisites, first steps
- **[Architecture](./architecture.md)** ‚Äî Output format, confidence model, tiers, tool ecosystem
- **[Agents](./agents.md)** ‚Äî Ferris agent: modes, capabilities, communication style
- **[Workflows](./workflows.md)** ‚Äî All 10 workflows with commands and connection diagram
- **[Examples](./examples.md)** ‚Äî Real-world scenarios, tips, and troubleshooting

## Support

- **Issues**: [GitHub Issues](https://github.com/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/issues)

---
title: Getting Started
description: Installation, prerequisites, first steps, and common use cases for Skill Forge
---

# Getting Started with SKF: Skill Forge

Welcome to Skill Forge! This guide will help you get up and running.

---

## What This Module Does

Skill Forge is an automated skill compiler for the AI agent ecosystem. It transforms code repositories, documentation websites, and developer discourse into agentskills.io-compliant, version-pinned, provenance-backed agent skills. Every instruction traces to actual code ‚Äî zero hallucination tolerance.

---

## Installation

If you haven't installed the module yet:

```bash
npx bmad-module-skill-forge install
```

Follow the prompts to configure the module for your needs.

---

## Prerequisites

| Tool                                                                   | Required For       | Install                     |
|------------------------------------------------------------------------|--------------------|-----------------------------|
| `gh` (GitHub CLI)                                                      | All modes          | <https://cli.github.com>      |
| `ast-grep`  (CLI tool for code structural search, lint, and rewriting) | Forge + Deep modes | <https://ast-grep.github.io>  |
| `qmd` (local hybrid search engine for markdown)                        | Deep mode          | <https://github.com/tobi/qmd> |

Don't worry if you don't have all tools ‚Äî SKF detects what's available and sets your tier automatically.

---

## First Steps

### 1. Setup Your Forge

```
@Ferris SF
```

This detects your tools, sets your capability tier, and initializes the forge environment. You only need to do this once per project.

### 2. Generate Your First Skill

**Fastest path (Quick Skill):**
```
@Ferris QS https://github.com/bmad-code-org/BMAD-METHOD
```

Ferris reads the repository, extracts the public API, and generates a skill in under a minute.

**Full quality path:**
```
@Ferris BS    # Brief ‚Äî scope and design the skill
@Ferris CS    # Create ‚Äî compile from the brief
@Ferris TS    # Test ‚Äî verify completeness
@Ferris EX    # Export ‚Äî package for distribution
```

### 3. Stack Skill (for full projects)

```
@Ferris SS
```

Analyzes your project's dependencies and generates a consolidated stack skill with integration patterns.

---

## Common Use Cases

### My agent keeps hallucinating API calls

Your agent invents function signatures that don't exist. Generate a verified skill so it works from structural truth instead of guessing.

```
@Ferris QS https://github.com/org/library
```

The skill pins every function to its actual source location. Hallucinations stop.

### I'm adopting a new library and need my agent to use it correctly

You added a dependency but your agent doesn't know its API yet. Quick Skill resolves package names across npm, PyPI, and crates.io.

```
@Ferris QS cognee
```

Ferris resolves the package to its GitHub repo, extracts the public API, and generates a skill your agent can reference immediately.

### I want my agent to understand my entire project stack

Individual skills cover single libraries. Stack Skill maps how your dependencies interact ‚Äî shared types, co-import patterns, integration points.

```
@Ferris SS
```

Ferris detects your manifests, ranks dependencies by significance, and generates a consolidated skill with cross-library integration patterns.

### I'm onboarding a large existing codebase

A brownfield repo with dozens of modules. You need to know what's worth skilling before you start.

```
@Ferris AN
```

Analyze Source scans the project, identifies skillable units, maps exports, and generates recommended briefs you can batch-create with `@Ferris CS --batch`.

### I want the highest accuracy possible

Quick mode reads source files. Forge mode adds AST parsing for structural truth. Deep mode goes further ‚Äî QMD indexes your project's markdown (docs, changelogs, issues, migration guides) into searchable collections, then uses BM25 keyword search, vector semantic search, and LLM-powered re-ranking to surface context no other tool can find.

```
@Ferris SF    # Setup detects your tools and sets tier automatically
```

If `ast-grep` and `qmd` are available, Ferris activates Deep mode. Every skill gets AST-verified signatures (T1) enriched with deep knowledge search (T2) ‚Äî deprecation warnings, breaking change history, common pitfalls surfaced by hybrid search across your indexed documentation.

### I maintain an OSS library and want official skills

You want maximum quality with full provenance for distribution. Use the full pipeline with Deep mode for the richest output.

```
@Ferris BS    # Scope and design the skill
@Ferris CS    # Compile ‚Äî AST extraction + QMD enrichment (Deep)
@Ferris TS    # Verify completeness before publishing
@Ferris EX    # Package for distribution
```

Export with `source_authority: official`. Consumers get verified skills that update with each release.

### A dependency shipped breaking changes

Your existing skill is now out of date. Audit detects the drift, Update regenerates while preserving your manual additions.

```
@Ferris AS    # Find what changed
@Ferris US    # Regenerate ‚Äî [MANUAL] sections survive
@Ferris TS    # Verify the update
```

---

## What's Next?

- Check out the [Agents Reference](../agents.md) to learn about Ferris
- Browse the [Workflows Reference](../workflows.md) to see all available commands
- See [Examples](../examples.md) for real-world usage scenarios

---

## Need Help?

If you run into issues:
1. Run `/bmad-help` ‚Äî analyzes your current state and suggests what to do next
   (e.g. `/bmad-help my quick skill has low confidence scores, how do I improve them?`)
2. Run `@Ferris SF` to check your tool availability and tier
3. Check `forge-config.yaml` for your current configuration

## Install

```bash
npx bmad-module-skill-forge install
```

input.new_string:
## Install

There are three ways to install SKF, depending on your setup.

### Method 1: Standalone (recommended for trying SKF)

```bash
npx bmad-module-skill-forge install
```

Installs SKF on its own. You'll be prompted for project name, output folders, and which IDEs to configure. The installer generates IDE-specific command files (e.g. `.claude/commands/`, `.cursor/commands/`) so workflows appear in your IDE's command palette.

### Method 2: With BMad Method (recommended for full workflow)

```bash
npx bmad-method install
```

During module selection, choose **SKF: Skill Forge**. This installs BMad core + SKF together with full IDE integration, manifests, and help catalog. Best when you want the complete BMad development workflow.

### Method 3: Add SKF to an existing BMad project

If you already have BMad installed via `npx bmad-method install`, you can add SKF afterward by running the standalone installer in the same directory:

```bash
npx bmad-module-skill-forge install
```

The installer detects the existing `_bmad/` directory and installs SKF alongside your current modules. IDE command files are generated for SKF workflows.

# Skill Forge (SKF) SKF (Skill Forge) is a standalone BMAD module that transforms code repositories, documentation websites, and developer discourse into agentskills.io-compliant, version-pinned, provenance-backed agent skills. It provides a single expert agent (Ferris, Skill Architect & Integrity Guardian) and ten workflows spanning source analysis, skill briefing, AST-backed compilation, integrity testing, and ecosystem-ready export across progressive capability tiers (Quick/Forge/Deep). Docs: ## Why SKF - AST-verified instructions with line-level provenance ‚Äî no hallucinated guidance - Progressive capability model that meets developers where they are - Full lifecycle: discover, brief, compile, test, audit, update, export - Version-pinned skills that track source changes and detect drift - Zero hallucination tolerance ‚Äî every instruction traces to code ## How BMad Works BMad works because it turns big, fuzzy work into **repeatable workflows**. Each workflow is broken into small steps with clear instructions, so the AI follows the same path every time. It also uses a **shared knowledge base** (standards and patterns) so outputs are consistent, not random. In short: **structured steps + shared standards = reliable results**. ## How SKF Fits In SKF plugs into BMad the same way a specialist plugs into a team. It uses the same step-by-step workflow engine and shared standards, but focuses exclusively on skill compilation and quality assurance. That means you get **evidence-based agent skills**, **AST-verified instructions**, and **drift detection** that align with the rest of the BMad process. ## Architecture & Flow BMad is a small **agent + workflow engine**. There is no external orchestrator ‚Äî everything runs inside the LLM context window through structured instructions. ### Building Blocks Each workflow directory contains these files, and each has a specific job: | File | What it does | When it loads | | --- | --- | --- | | `skf.agent.yaml` | Expert persona ‚Äî identity, principles, critical actions, menu of triggers | First ‚Äî always in context | | `workflow.md` | Human-readable entry point ‚Äî goals, mode menu (Create/Edit/Validate), routes to first step | Second ‚Äî presents mode choice | | `steps-c/*.md` | **Create** steps ‚Äî primary execution, 4-9 sequential files | One at a time (just-in-time) | | `data/*.md` | Workflow-specific reference data ‚Äî schemas, heuristics, rules, patterns | Read by steps on demand | | `templates/*.md` | Output skeletons with placeholder vars ‚Äî steps fill these in to produce the final artifact | Read by steps when generating output | | `skf-knowledge-index.csv` | Knowledge fragment index ‚Äî id, name, tags, tier, file path | Read by steps to decide which fragments to load | | `knowledge/*.md` | 8 reusable fragments ‚Äî cross-cutting principles and patterns (e.g., `zero-hallucination.md`, `confidence-tiers.md`) | Selectively read into context when a step directs | ```mermaid flowchart LR U[User] --> A[Agent Persona] A --> W[Workflow Entry: workflow.md] W --> S[Step Files: steps-c/] S --> K[Knowledge Fragments
skf-knowledge-index.csv ‚Üí knowledge/*.md] S --> D[Data & Templates
data/*.md, templates/*.md] S --> O[Outputs: skills/reports
when a step writes output] ``` ### How It Works at Runtime 1. **Trigger** ‚Äî User types `@Ferris CS` (or fuzzy match like `create-skill`). The agent menu in `skf.agent.yaml` maps the trigger to the workflow path. 2. **Agent loads** ‚Äî `skf.agent.yaml` injects the persona (identity, principles, critical actions) into the context window. Sidecar files (`forge-tier.yaml`, `preferences.yaml`) are loaded for persistent state. 3. **Workflow loads** ‚Äî `workflow.md` presents the mode choice and routes to the first step file. 4. **Step-by-step execution** ‚Äî Only the current step file is in context (just-in-time loading). Each step explicitly names the next one. The LLM reads, executes, saves output, then loads the next step. No future steps are ever preloaded. 5. **Knowledge injection** ‚Äî Steps consult `skf-knowledge-index.csv` and selectively load fragments from `knowledge/` by tags and relevance. Cross-cutting principles (zero hallucination, confidence tiers, provenance) are loaded only when a step directs ‚Äî not preloaded. 6. **Data injection** ‚Äî Steps read `data/*.md` files as needed (schemas, heuristics, extraction patterns). This is deliberate context engineering: only the data relevant to the current step enters the context window. 7. **Templates** ‚Äî When a step produces output (e.g., a skill brief or test report), it reads the template file and fills in placeholders with computed results. The template provides consistent structure; the step provides the content. 8. **Progress tracking** ‚Äî Each step appends to an output file with state tracking. Resume mode reads this state and routes to the next incomplete step. ### Ferris Operating Modes Ferris operates in four workflow-driven modes (mode is determined by which workflow is running, not conversation state): | Mode | Workflows | Behavior | | --- | --- | --- | | **Architect** | AN, BS, SF | Exploratory, assembling ‚Äî discovers structure and scope | | **Surgeon** | CS, QS, SS, US | Precise, preserving ‚Äî extracts and compiles with provenance | | **Audit** | AS, TS | Judgmental, scoring ‚Äî evaluates quality and detects drift | | **Delivery** | EX | Packaging, ecosystem-ready ‚Äî bundles for distribution | ## Install ```bash npx bmad-module-skill-forge install ``` You'll be prompted for: - **Skills output folder** ‚Äî Where generated skills are saved (default: `skills/`) - **Forge data folder** ‚Äî Where workspace artifacts are stored (default: `forge-data/`) ## Quickstart 1. **Setup your forge:** `@Ferris SF` ‚Äî detects tools, sets your tier (Quick/Forge/Deep) 2. **Quick skill (fastest):** `@Ferris QS ` ‚Äî fast skill from a package name 3. **Full skill:** `@Ferris BS` then `@Ferris CS` ‚Äî brief then compile for maximum quality 4. **Stack skill:** `@Ferris SS` ‚Äî consolidated project stack skill with integration patterns 5. **Export:** `@Ferris EX` ‚Äî package for distribution, update CLAUDE.md ## Workflows | Trigger | Command | Purpose | | --- | --- | --- | | SF | `skf_setup_forge` | Initialize forge environment, detect tools, set tier | | AN | `skf_analyze_source` | Discover what to skill in a large repo | | BS | `skf_brief_skill` | Design a skill scope through guided discovery | | CS | `skf_create_skill` | Compile a skill from brief (supports --batch) | | QS | `skf_quick_skill` | Fast skill from package name or GitHub URL | | SS | `skf_create_stack_skill` | Consolidated project stack skill with integration patterns | | US | `skf_update_skill` | Smart regeneration preserving \[MANUAL\] sections | | AS | `skf_audit_skill` | Drift detection between skill and current source | | TS | `skf_test_skill` | Cognitive completeness verification ‚Äî quality gate before export | | EX | `skf_export_skill` | Package for distribution, inject into CLAUDE.md/AGENTS.md | ## Progressive Capability Model | Tier | Tools | Capability | | --- | --- | --- | | **Quick** | gh + skills_ref | Source reading + spec validation | | **Forge** | + ast-grep | Structural truth, T1 confidence | | **Deep** | + QMD | Knowledge search, temporal provenance | The `setup-forge` workflow detects available tools and writes the tier to `forge-tier.yaml`. All subsequent workflows adapt their behavior to the detected tier. ## Knowledge Base SKF relies on a curated skill compilation knowledge base: - Index: `src/knowledge/skf-knowledge-index.csv` - Fragments: `src/knowledge/` Workflows load only the fragments required for the current task to stay focused and compliant. ## Configuration SKF variables are defined in `src/module.yaml` and prompted during install: | Variable | Purpose | Default | | --- | --- | --- | | `skills_output_folder` | Where generated skills are saved | `{project-root}/skills` | | `forge_data_folder` | Where workspace artifacts are stored | `{project-root}/forge-data` | Runtime configuration (tool detection, tier, parallel settings) is managed by the `setup-forge` workflow in `forge-tier.yaml`. ## Module Structure ``` src/ ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ module.yaml ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ module-help.csv ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ README.md ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ docs/ ‚îÇ ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ getting-started.md ‚îÇ ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ agents.md ‚îÇ ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ workflows.md ‚îÇ ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ examples.md ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ agents/ ‚îÇ ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ skf.agent.yaml ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ ferris/ ‚îÇ ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ forge-tier.yaml ‚îÇ ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ preferences.yaml ‚îÇ ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ README.md ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ knowledge/ ‚îÇ ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ skf-knowledge-index.csv ‚îÇ ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ *.md (8 fragments) ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ workflows/ ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ skillforge/ ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ setup-forge/ ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ analyze-source/ ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ brief-skill/ ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ create-skill/ ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ quick-skill/ ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ create-stack-skill/ ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ update-skill/ ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ audit-skill/ ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ test-skill/ ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ export-skill/ ``` ## Contributing See [CONTRIBUTORS.md](CONTRIBUTORS.md) for guidelines. ## License MIT License ‚Äî see [LICENSE](LICENSE) for details. --- **Skill Forge (SKF)** ‚Äî A standalone [BMad](https://github.com/bmad-code-org/BMAD-METHOD) module for agent skill compilation. [![Contributors](https://contrib.rocks/image?repo=armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge)](https://github.com/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/graphs/contributors) See [CONTRIBUTORS.md](CONTRIBUTORS.md) for contributor information.

---
title: Getting Started
description: Installation, prerequisites, first steps, and common use cases for Skill Forge
---

# Getting Started with SKF: Skill Forge

Welcome to Skill Forge! This guide will help you get up and running.

---

## What This Module Does

Skill Forge is an automated skill compiler for the AI agent ecosystem. It transforms code repositories, documentation websites, and developer discourse into agentskills.io-compliant, version-pinned, provenance-backed agent skills. Every instruction traces to actual code ‚Äî zero hallucination tolerance.

---

## Installation

If you haven't installed the module yet:

```bash
npx bmad-module-skill-forge install
```

Follow the prompts to configure the module for your needs.

---

## Prerequisites

| Tool                                                                   | Required For       | Install                     |
|------------------------------------------------------------------------|--------------------|-----------------------------|
| `gh` (GitHub CLI)                                                      | All modes          | <https://cli.github.com>      |
| `ast-grep`  (CLI tool for code structural search, lint, and rewriting) | Forge + Deep modes | <https://ast-grep.github.io>  |
| `qmd` (local hybrid search engine for markdown)                        | Deep mode          | <https://github.com/tobi/qmd> |

Don't worry if you don't have all tools ‚Äî SKF detects what's available and sets your tier automatically.

---

## First Steps

### 1. Setup Your Forge

```
@Ferris SF
```

This detects your tools, sets your capability tier, and initializes the forge environment. You only need to do this once per project.

### 2. Generate Your First Skill

**Fastest path (Quick Skill):**
```
@Ferris QS https://github.com/bmad-code-org/BMAD-METHOD
```

Ferris reads the repository, extracts the public API, and generates a skill in under a minute.

**Full quality path:**
```
@Ferris BS    # Brief ‚Äî scope and design the skill
@Ferris CS    # Create ‚Äî compile from the brief
@Ferris TS    # Test ‚Äî verify completeness
@Ferris EX    # Export ‚Äî package for distribution
```

### 3. Stack Skill (for full projects)

```
@Ferris SS
```

Analyzes your project's dependencies and generates a consolidated stack skill with integration patterns.

---

## Common Use Cases

### My agent keeps hallucinating API calls

Your agent invents function signatures that don't exist. Generate a verified skill so it works from structural truth instead of guessing.

```
@Ferris QS https://github.com/org/library
```

The skill pins every function to its actual source location. Hallucinations stop.

### I'm adopting a new library and need my agent to use it correctly

You added a dependency but your agent doesn't know its API yet. Quick Skill resolves package names across npm, PyPI, and crates.io.

```
@Ferris QS cognee
```

Ferris resolves the package to its GitHub repo, extracts the public API, and generates a skill your agent can reference immediately.

### I want my agent to understand my entire project stack

Individual skills cover single libraries. Stack Skill maps how your dependencies interact ‚Äî shared types, co-import patterns, integration points.

```
@Ferris SS
```

Ferris detects your manifests, ranks dependencies by significance, and generates a consolidated skill with cross-library integration patterns.

### I'm onboarding a large existing codebase

A brownfield repo with dozens of modules. You need to know what's worth skilling before you start.

```
@Ferris AN
```

Analyze Source scans the project, identifies skillable units, maps exports, and generates recommended briefs you can batch-create with `@Ferris CS --batch`.

### I want the highest accuracy possible

Quick mode reads source files. Forge mode adds AST parsing for structural truth. Deep mode goes further ‚Äî QMD indexes your project's markdown (docs, changelogs, issues, migration guides) into searchable collections, then uses BM25 keyword search, vector semantic search, and LLM-powered re-ranking to surface context no other tool can find.

```
@Ferris SF    # Setup detects your tools and sets tier automatically
```

If `ast-grep` and `qmd` are available, Ferris activates Deep mode. Every skill gets AST-verified signatures (T1) enriched with deep knowledge search (T2) ‚Äî deprecation warnings, breaking change history, common pitfalls surfaced by hybrid search across your indexed documentation.

### I maintain an OSS library and want official skills

You want maximum quality with full provenance for distribution. Use the full pipeline with Deep mode for the richest output.

```
@Ferris BS    # Scope and design the skill
@Ferris CS    # Compile ‚Äî AST extraction + QMD enrichment (Deep)
@Ferris TS    # Verify completeness before publishing
@Ferris EX    # Package for distribution
```

Export with `source_authority: official`. Consumers get verified skills that update with each release.

### A dependency shipped breaking changes

Your existing skill is now out of date. Audit detects the drift, Update regenerates while preserving your manual additions.

```
@Ferris AS    # Find what changed
@Ferris US    # Regenerate ‚Äî [MANUAL] sections survive
@Ferris TS    # Verify the update
```

---

## What's Next?

- Check out the [Agents Reference](../agents.md) to learn about Ferris
- Browse the [Workflows Reference](../workflows.md) to see all available commands
- See [Examples](../examples.md) for real-world usage scenarios

---

## Need Help?

If you run into issues:
1. Run `/bmad-help` ‚Äî analyzes your current state and suggests what to do next
   (e.g. `/bmad-help my quick skill has low confidence scores, how do I improve them?`)
2. Run `@Ferris SF` to check your tool availability and tier
3. Check `forge-config.yaml` for your current configuration

## What is Skill Forge?

Skill Forge is an automated skill compiler for the AI agent ecosystem. It transforms code repositories, documentation, and developer discourse into [agentskills.io](https://agentskills.io)-compliant, version-pinned, provenance-backed agent skills. Every instruction traces to verifiable sources ‚Äî zero hallucination tolerance.

- **AST-Verified**: Structural truth via ast-grep ‚Äî no guessing, no hallucination.
- **Provenance-Backed**: Every claim traces to source code with file and line references.
- **Progressive Tiers**: Quick (no setup) ‚Üí Forge (ast-grep) ‚Üí Deep (QMD knowledge).
- **Ecosystem-First**: Checks for official skills before generating community ones.

## Quick Install

```bash
npx bmad-module-skill-forge install
```

Then interact with the forge agent:

```
@Ferris SF    # Setup your forge environment
@Ferris QS    # Quick Skill ‚Äî generate in under a minute
```

## Core Workflows

| Workflow | Trigger | Purpose |
|----------|---------|---------|
| [Setup Forge](/workflows/#setup-forge-sf) | SF | Initialize forge, detect tools, set tier |
| [Brief Skill](/workflows/#brief-skill-bs) | BS | Scope and design a skill |
| [Create Skill](/workflows/#create-skill-cs) | CS | Compile a skill from a brief |
| [Quick Skill](/workflows/#quick-skill-qs) | QS | Fast skill, no brief needed |
| [Stack Skill](/workflows/#stack-skill-ss) | SS | Consolidated project stack skill |
| [Update Skill](/workflows/#update-skill-us) | US | Regenerate after source changes |
| [Audit Skill](/workflows/#audit-skill-as) | AS | Drift detection |
| [Test Skill](/workflows/#test-skill-ts) | TS | Verify completeness |
| [Export Skill](/workflows/#export-skill-ex) | EX | Package for distribution |
| [Analyze Source](/workflows/#analyze-source-an) | AN | Discover what to skill |

## Documentation

- **[Getting Started](./getting-started.md)** ‚Äî Installation, prerequisites, first steps
- **[Architecture](./architecture.md)** ‚Äî Output format, confidence model, tiers, tool ecosystem
- **[Agents](./agents.md)** ‚Äî Ferris agent: modes, capabilities, communication style
- **[Workflows](./workflows.md)** ‚Äî All 10 workflows with commands and connection diagram
- **[Examples](./examples.md)** ‚Äî Real-world scenarios, tips, and troubleshooting

## Support

- **Issues**: [GitHub Issues](https://github.com/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/issues)

```bash
npx bmad-method install
```

During module selection, choose **SKF: Skill Forge**. This installs BMad core + SKF together with full IDE integration, manifests, and help catalog. Best when you want the complete BMad development workflow.

### Add SKF to an existing BMad project

If you already have BMad installed, you can add SKF afterward by running the standalone installer in the same directory:

```bash
npx bmad-module-skill-forge install
```

The installer detects the existing `_bmad/` directory and installs SKF alongside your current modules.

file.content:
---
title: Getting Started
description: Installation, prerequisites, first steps, and common use cases for Skill Forge
---

# Getting Started with SKF: Skill Forge

Welcome to Skill Forge! This guide will help you get up and running.

---

## What This Module Does

Skill Forge is an automated skill compiler for the AI agent ecosystem. It transforms code repositories, documentation websites, and developer discourse into agentskills.io-compliant, version-pinned, provenance-backed agent skills. Every instruction traces to actual code ‚Äî zero hallucination tolerance.

---

## Installation

There are three ways to install SKF, depending on your setup.

### Standalone (recommended for trying SKF)

```bash
npx bmad-module-skill-forge install
```

Installs SKF on its own. You'll be prompted for project name, output folders, and which IDEs to configure. The installer generates IDE-specific command files (e.g. `.claude/commands/`, `.cursor/commands/`) so workflows appear in your IDE's command palette.

### With BMad Method (recommended for full workflow)
